speaker: interviewer
text: We'll try and finish up here. Tell me about how you first came to this country, and where you went, and what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was invited by my girlfriend from New York-- that was my school girlfriend, Martha-- to Switzerland in 1968. And at the time, I was married to a-- I had a good, pretty good marriage with a Polish man. And I had a father in Poland. But it was becoming unpleasant-- that was 1968-- for many reasons. And my girlfriend wanted to have vacation in Switzerland. She was a resident, in New York, of pathology. We went to medical school together, and to gymnasium or whatever, high school together. And so she asked-- she sent me a ticket to Switzerland by train--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for me and my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that you want to-- don't particularly want to talk about the period from after the war to that. But what did you-- I mean, what did you do as an adult in, in, in Poland? Did you-- Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father took me from children's home, sent me to a school which was mostly for kids who were not having any religion at school. And they mostly, of these kids, were Jewish. So I didn't really had anti-Semitic kids at school. They were mostly Jews from Russia. And I became friends with one of the girl, who was a good-- who was Jewish. And I had two or three good friends. And so we went to America. I went to medicine. I graduated from medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1960. And I was married. I married very early.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your husband Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And we had problems with getting apartment. I met my husband at school when I was 16. And I-- we had one daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Halina, like my mother. And I, and Halina, was invited by my friend, Martha, as I mentioned. And then was the war between Poland and Czechoslovakia. And I was invited just for summer, but I went to Jewish organization-- Joint-- in Switzerland, and asked them if they could help me and my daughter. No, first I called my father. And my father was always wanted me to leave Poland. He said there is no future for me in Poland. And he was always feeling bad and depressed that he came to-- you know, that he and my mother didn't leave Poland before the war. And he lost everybody, all his family. He was very depressed man. Chronically depressed man, very pessimistic. And he kind of pushed me. He said-- when I called him-- he said, I hope you will not return. And I asked that the Jewish Joint organization to help me and my daughter, to ask for asylum, and to-- to leave Poland. Well, they-- they directed me to Italy, [SIGHS] me and my daughter, and they really helped me to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of doctor were you when you were in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was a psychiatrist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very difficult to get a job. But I somehow managed to get a job in cardiovascular research, in Cedars-- old Cedars on Fountain. And I was paid very little. And I rented a little place on Edgemont Street. I didn't know how to drive a car. And my daughter started school. She was about seven. And I met my second husband, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lauterbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: First? Otto. O-T-T-O. I have pictures with him, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father helped me with the divorce-- he was a lawyer-- from my first husband. And I remarried, and just very soon after I came to this country, maybe one year after. And we-- my second husband, Otto Lauterbach, adopted my daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wasn't religious, either. I'm sorry. [LAUGHS] We did celebrate some Jewish holidays, but, in his heart, he did it for tradition, or whatever, only for family. And unfortunately, on Yom Kippur, he was always getting hungry. [LAUGHTER] So he ate. So if it comes to religious background, it's not too much. But Otto Lauterbach was a very good man. And people just love him as a doctor. And when he was sick, they made testimonial dinner for him. There were like 250 people. And there were Poles and Americans and Jews, because he treated them all. He was a family doctor. And he had a speech about relationship of Poles and Jews, how some were Poles are really heroes, who helped, and how-- you know, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you mentioned you have patients. What kind of-- what are you-- what kind of doctor are you these days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I am a psychiatrist.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you continued that work here, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS] I have three-year-old grandson, and my daughter is expecting in-- she-- well, in some times-- maybe eight months-- [LAUGHS] seven months. She just let me know. She wanted to have another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your grandchild's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Larman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we look at your pictures, is there anything that you want to say that I didn't ask about, or that you might have--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure that things will come to my head, that I forgot to tell you this and I forgot to tell you this, and this was important, and I forgot to tell-- I mean, that's usually, I think, when people tell their story.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't have anything in your head now that you-- that's come-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] that you would like to close with?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hmm. [SIGHS] Well, I-- I do really feel so strongly that-- that people are the same, independently on-- on their nationality, or, you know. I even don't believe that the Jews are, you know, better, or blacks are not as intelligent. But I understand that there always will be tension between minorities, or people who are a little bit different will always be not treated equally. But what I really will always remember is that the Germans are particular people because, to me, you can be very cruel, and you can really kill others. But what I think they had as superiority is their German organization. They are terribly well [LAUGHS] organized, if you can use terrible with organization. Their cars, probably-- I wouldn't drive their cars-- but probably Porsche and Mercedes are very good cars. And they did make this Holocaust such an organized experience. Like, they knew how to give the numbers. They knew that people were in lines to go to concentration camps. They knew what day they will kill children, and how they will make soap of them. And the-- the organization, I think, it's one thing which I really resent. And I was offered a lot of money by Germans when I was the poorest, when I really needed, when I left Poland, for just living there. Because my grandparents and my uncle were German Jews. And it was pretty easy money I could get, just by returning to my country, being-- being German. They-- they offered me quite good reparation for the possessions we had in Danzig, for whatever they-- they would pay, because I survived and suffered because of them. And I feel quite proud of myself that I refused them. And that's it. [LAUGHS] I don't know. I just made this statement. I-- well, I didn't expect you will ask. [LAUGHS] Wasn't prepared.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. That's the picture of my father, made in 1956. And that's when he-- it's the certificate that he belongs to association of lawyers in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jerzy Majzel, M-A-Y-Z-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was sent to my father by my mother in 1940. It was taken at the beginning of ghetto, when we still could manage, and my grandparents were alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Halina Majzel, and her maiden name is Korenberg. This is me, taken in Warsaw in 1943, and I was taking Communion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Borzenka Goschinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I am at the same time, when I had my Communion, 1943, with Kaluza family. I have-- my hair is starting to grow straight, like in a bang. After this prostitute, I was shaved my hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can--

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there is young generation off-- the son and two daughter of Mrs. Kaluza. And I am the girl with the bang. And that's Ola, me, in children's home after the war. It's about 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm in children's home, in a Jewish children's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was out of Warsaw. It was [? Otvosk. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken about 1979. And my husband liked it, and it was in his office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me who it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. That's me about 15 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are? Ola?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Ola. [INAUDIBLE]. That's Ola-- me-- and my husband, Otto. And it was taken when we went to visit our daughter, Halina, who was in the camp for Jewish kids. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was about 1972 or 1973.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken, Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think it was Arrowhead, or someplace like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband and my daughter in 1971. We were living, at this time, in Park La Brea, Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their-- what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Halina and Otto. That's my daughter and my son-in-law when they had their modest wedding in San Francisco. His name is Hugo Larman, and her name is Halina Lauterbach, presently Larman. Thank you. This is Daniel Larman. He's one year and seven months old, in his father's shoes, playing cowboy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in his home in San Francisco.
